I want to be honest with you about something. For years, when someone asked me a hard question in English, I just froze. My mind went blank, and the silence felt huge. I thought I had to answer perfectly and instantly, so when I could not, I panicked. Then a teacher told me something simple that changed everything. She said: you do not have to be fast, you have to stay calm. Buy yourself a little time. So now, when I am unsure, I do not freeze. I say, that is a good question, let me think for a second. Or, honestly, I am not sure, but I would guess it is around twenty. Frankly, those small phrases are not weak. They are confident. They tell the other person, I am thinking, please wait. And here is the trick I love the most. When I want to keep the conversation soft and friendly, I add a little question tag at the end. It is useful, isn't it? You have felt this too, haven't you? That tiny tag invites the other person in, and suddenly I am not alone with the pressure. The conversation breathes.

Let me tell you about a moment in a meeting last month. My manager turned to me and asked, so what are the exact numbers for this quarter? Everyone was looking at me, and I did not have the precise figure in my head. A year ago, I would have panicked. This time, I stayed calm. I said, that is a fair question. Let me think for a second. Then I gave a vague but honest answer. I said, I do not have the exact number in front of me, but roughly, it is around forty thousand. I would estimate we are up about ten percent. Then I added a tag to check we were on the same page. We can confirm the details later, can't we? My manager nodded. Here is what I learned. Nobody expects you to know everything instantly. What they respect is someone who stays calm, gives an honest estimate, and is not afraid to say, I will get back to you with the exact figure. Buying time is not a weakness. Frankly, it is a sign of a confident professional.

First, when I do not know the answer right away, I stay calm instead of freezing. Then I buy a little time and say, that is a good question, let me think for a second. After that, I give an honest answer and admit I am unsure: honestly, I am not sure, but roughly it is around twenty. Next, I soften it and check with a question tag, so it is about twenty, isn't it? In the end, if I really do not know, I promise to follow up and say, I will get back to you with the exact figure.
